sketch: VaultCore ships as two SwiftPM-visible layers over one on-disk vault. The ciphertext plane is a `SealedVault` type constructed from just a directory URL: it can enumerate `chunks/{hash}`, verify each blob's BLAKE2b address matches its content, parse `gallery.meta` structurally (salt, argon2 params, epoch, wrapped_dek present and well-formed), copy chunks in/out for sync (receive = hash-verify then link into CAS; send = stream raw ciphertext), and run a public `fsck`/FormatVerifier that walks the whole layout â€” all without a DEK, so the CLI peer, backup jobs, and the future sync engine operate on locked vaults. The plaintext plane is only reachable by calling `SealedVault.unlock(password:)`, which runs Argon2id, unwraps the DEK, and returns an `UnlockedSession` value that owns the DEK in memory; every decrypt/encrypt API (chunk-range reads, import, manifest decryption) is a method on `UnlockedSession` or takes a token only it can mint, so 'locked vs unlocked' is a compile-time reachability fact â€” there is no `isUnlocked` boolean to check or forget to check. Locking is `session.invalidate()` (zeroize DEK, poison the token); anything holding a stale token gets a thrown `SessionInvalidated`, not silent plaintext. AEAD-tag verification stays on the plaintext plane (it needs the DEK), which is exactly the split the spec wants: the ciphertext plane proves 'the bytes are the bytes' (address integrity, structural validity) while the plaintext plane proves 'the bytes are authentic' (Poly1305 under the DEK). This maps cleanly onto the goal's green gates: the no-plaintext-on-disk sentinel test only needs to audit the plaintext plane, and the sync/CLI legs get a stable, key-free API surface that never grows a footgun.
risk: AEAD tag verification requires the DEK, so the ciphertext plane's fsck can only prove address integrity (BLAKE2b(ciphertext) == filename) and structural validity â€” it cannot detect a chunk whose hash was recomputed after tampering plus a manifest swap, and more practically it can silently pass a vault whose chunks are internally consistent but undecryptable (wrong-epoch, corrupted-before-hashing). If the API names imply 'verified' too strongly, sync and backup tooling will treat ciphertext-plane green as end-to-end integrity, which it is not; the two verification tiers must be named and documented as distinct (e.g. `auditAddresses()` vs `session.verifyAuthenticity()`), or the split leaks a false-confidence bug into every downstream consumer.
first_step: Define the two public types in a fresh `Sources/VaultCore/`: `SealedVault` (init from directory URL; `chunkHashes()`, `readCiphertext(hash:)`, `receiveChunk(data:)` with hash-check, `auditAddresses()`, `readMetaHeader()`) and `UnlockedSession` (only obtainable via `SealedVault.unlock(password:) throws -> UnlockedSession`; holds the DEK; `decryptRange(fileID:chunks:)`, `importFile(...)`, `invalidate()`), then write the first test proving the type system enforces the split: a target that only imports the ciphertext-plane symbols compiles and round-trips a chunk copy against a locked fixture vault with no password anywhere in scope.
children: Make `UnlockedSession` a `~Copyable` (non-copyable) Swift type so DEK ownership is move-only: `invalidate()` is a consuming func, the compiler statically prevents use-after-lock, and zeroization has exactly one owner â€” turning the capability-token idea into a borrow-checker guarantee instead of a runtime poison flag.

Ciphertext-plane sync transcript: since `receiveChunk`/`sendChunk` need no key, define a tiny serialized 'have/want' diff format on the sealed plane now (hash-set exchange from spec Â§8.3) so the future CRDT sync leg and the CLI peer can be built and integration-tested entirely against locked fixture vaults.

Two-tier fsck as a product feature: `vaultcore fsck` (CLI, sealed plane) checks addresses/layout on a NAS backup without ever holding a password, while `fsck --deep` prompts to unlock and adds AEAD verification â€” explicitly naming the tiers kills the false-confidence risk and gives the Linux peer its first useful command.

Sealed-plane thumbnail/manifest opacity budget: because the ciphertext plane can enumerate sizes and counts of chunks, deliberately pad or bucket chunk-count metadata exposed by inventory APIs so a locked vault leaks minimal shape information (photo count, video length) to whoever holds the ciphertext â€” an API-design decision that is nearly free now and impossible to retrofit after the sync format ships.

Capability-token subsets: instead of one all-powerful `UnlockedSession`, let it mint narrower tokens â€” a read-only `DecryptToken` for the AVAssetResourceLoader playback path and a write-only `ImportToken` for the share-extension importer â€” so the streaming-playback leg never holds import capability and the app shell can hand the loader delegate the least capability that plays video.

Epoch-aware sealed plane: `readMetaHeader()` already exposes the reserved epoch integer without a key, which means the future DEK-rotation leg gets migration tooling for free â€” a sealed-plane API can inventory which chunks belong to which epoch (via per-chunk epoch tags in the layout) and plan a re-wrap sync without unlocking.
